metadata.title: The State of AI in 2025: Agents, Innovation, and Transformation
metadata.author: Alex Singla, Alexander Zuckerzewski, Larina E, Michael Choi, Bryce Hall, Tara Balakrishnan (QuantumBlack AI by McKinsey)

content: WHAT: McKinsey's 2025 Global Survey on the state of AI (1,993 respondents across 105 nations, fielded June 25–July 29, 2025) reveals that 88% of organizations now regularly use AI in at least one function, up from 78% a year ago.

CORE FINDING: Despite widespread AI adoption, most organizations remain in experimentation or piloting phases — only ~one-third have begun scaling AI enterprise-wide, and just 39% report any EBIT-level financial impact.

AI AGENTS EMERGING: 62% of respondents say their organizations are at least experimenting with AI agents, and 23% are already scaling agentic AI in at least one function — most heavily in IT and knowledge management.

HIGH PERFORMERS STAND OUT: The top ~6% of organizations (AI high performers) are 3x more likely to pursue transformative change, fundamentally redesign workflows, have senior leader ownership, and commit 20%+ of digital budgets to AI.

WORKFORCE UNCERTAINTY: 32% of respondents expect enterprise-wide workforce reductions of 3%+ in the next year due to AI, while 43% expect no change and 13% expect increases — signaling significant but unresolved employment disruption ahead.

content.point: AI use is widespread but shallow: 88% of organizations use AI in at least one function, yet nearly two-thirds have NOT begun scaling it across the enterprise — the pilot-to-scale transition is stalled for most.

AI agents are gaining traction fast: 62% of respondents' organizations are at least experimenting with AI agents, with agent use most developed in IT (service desk management) and knowledge management (deep research).

Enterprise financial impact is still rare: Only 39% report any EBIT impact attributable to AI, and most of those say less than 5% of EBIT is AI-driven — use-case-level cost and revenue gains exist but don't yet roll up to the bottom line.

Workflow redesign is the single most distinguishing success factor: AI high performers are nearly 3x as likely to have fundamentally redesigned workflows, and this practice 'had one of the strongest contributions to achieving meaningful business impact of all the factors tested.'

Senior leadership ownership is a critical differentiator: High performers are three times more likely to strongly agree that senior leaders demonstrate ownership of and commitment to AI initiatives, including actively role-modeling AI use.

content: Move beyond efficiency-only objectives: Set growth and innovation as explicit AI goals alongside cost reduction — organizations doing so report higher rates of customer satisfaction improvement, competitive differentiation, revenue growth, and market share change.

Prioritize workflow redesign over tool deployment: Fundamentally redesign individual workflows rather than simply layering AI on existing processes — this is identified as one of the strongest contributors to enterprise-level AI value.

Secure and visibly activate senior leadership commitment: Ensure senior leaders not only endorse AI initiatives but actively role-model AI use and drive adoption — high performers are 3x more likely to have this in place.

Invest proportionally: Commit more than 20% of digital budgets to AI technologies if targeting high-performer status — over one-third of high performers operate at this investment level, enabling faster scaling.

Establish human validation processes: Define clear processes for when and how model outputs require human review for accuracy — this practice is among the top factors distinguishing high performers from the rest.

Expand AI agent pilots into scaling in IT and knowledge management first: These are the functions with the most developed agentic use cases (service desk management, deep research), offering the clearest near-term path to agentic value.

strengths: Large, globally representative sample (1,993 respondents, 105 nations, weighted by GDP contribution) gives the findings credibility and reduces regional bias.

Longitudinal continuity: McKinsey has been running this AI survey for eight years, enabling genuine year-over-year comparisons (e.g., AI use up from 78% to 88%, risk mitigation managing average of 2 risks in 2022 vs. 4 today).

Actionable segmentation: The 'AI high performer' definition (~6% of respondents, 5%+ EBIT attributable to AI) creates a concrete benchmark and allows meaningful comparison of practices that differentiate top performers.
Covers a broad range of dimensions: strategy, talent, operating model, technology, data, adoption/scaling — consistent with McKinsey's 'Rewired' framework tested across 200+ at-scale transformations.
weaknesses: Self-reported survey data: All figures on EBIT impact, workflow redesign, and agent adoption are based on respondent perception, not verified financial or operational data — overstating or understating is possible.
The 'AI high performer' group represents only ~6% of respondents, making statistical conclusions about their behavior less robust and potentially skewed by a small cluster of outliers.

The audio-to-text conversion (automated TTS) introduces transcription artifacts (e.g., repeated words like 'profitability, profitability') that reduce precision and could obscure nuance in the original written report.
missing: No industry-specific breakdown is provided in the audio — the sidebar on industry-level AI adoption is referenced but not read out, leaving listeners without sector-specific context.

No qualitative case studies or named company examples are included — the findings remain statistical and abstract without concrete organizational stories to illustrate what 'transformative' AI use looks like in practice.
No discussion of how 'AI high performer' status is distributed across industries or geographies — it's unclear whether high performers cluster in tech or are spread broadly.
The methodology for weighting data by GDP contribution is mentioned but not explained — this could meaningfully affect findings given the overrepresentation of responses from certain regions.

thinking_framework.content: McKinsey implicitly applies a maturity-curve framework for AI adoption, structured across three stages: (1) Experimentation/Piloting — organizations trying AI in isolated use cases without enterprise integration; (2) Scaling — expanding AI into multiple functions with defined delivery processes; (3) Transformation — fundamentally redesigning workflows and treating AI as a business model catalyst. High performers are distinguished by six management dimensions from McKinsey's 'Rewired' research: Strategy, Talent, Operating Model, Technology, Data, and Adoption & Scaling. The framework positions workflow redesign and leadership commitment as the highest-leverage levers for moving up the maturity curve, ahead of technology investment alone.
thinking_framework.framework_name: AI Adoption Maturity Curve + Rewired Six-Dimension Management Framework

knowledge_insights.content: 1. The gap between use-case-level AI value and enterprise-level EBIT impact is the defining tension of 2025: organizations can see benefits in individual workflows (especially software engineering, manufacturing, IT for cost; marketing/sales and product development for revenue) but cannot yet aggregate these into measurable bottom-line performance. 2. AI agent adoption follows a function-first pattern — IT and knowledge management are the earliest-scaling domains because their use cases (service desk automation, deep research) are well-defined and self-contained. 3. Risk maturity is improving but asymmetric: organizations now mitigate an average of 4 AI risks (up from 2 in 2022), but explainability — the second most commonly experienced risk — is not among the most commonly mitigated, creating a governance blind spot. 4. Company size is a proxy for AI scaling readiness: nearly half of firms with $5B+ revenue are in the scaling phase vs. only 29% of firms under $100M — suggesting AI compounding benefits may widen the competitive gap between large and small enterprises. 5. Knowledge management has emerged as a top-three AI function for the first time, joining IT and marketing/sales — a shift reflecting the maturation of RAG-based and agentic research tools.

content: AI agents in IT: Service desk management is cited as a rapidly developed agentic use case within IT functions, representing one of the clearest early scaling examples of agentic AI across organizations.

AI agents in knowledge management: Deep research is cited as a primary agentic use case within knowledge management, now one of the top three functions for overall AI use — a new entrant alongside IT and marketing/sales.

Revenue impact by function: Marketing and sales, strategy and corporate finance, and product and service development are the functions most commonly reporting AI-driven revenue increases — consistent across multiple years of McKinsey survey data.
Cost impact by function: Software engineering, manufacturing, and IT are the functions most commonly reporting cost benefits from individual AI use cases.

High performer investment behavior: More than one-third of AI high performers commit more than 20% of their digital budgets to AI technologies, and approximately three-quarters are scaling or at-scale with AI — compared to one-third of other organizations.

Workforce impact example: Across business functions, a median of 17% of respondents reported workforce declines in the past year due to AI use, but a median of 30% expect declines in the coming year — a near-doubling of anticipated workforce reduction.

Risk consequence prevalence: 51% of respondents from AI-using organizations report at least one negative consequence, with nearly one-third specifically citing AI inaccuracy as the source — the most commonly experienced risk.

Pilot-to-scale transition is stalled: Nearly two-thirds of organizations have not yet begun scaling AI enterprise-wide — the gap between experimentation and systemic deployment remains the central challenge of 2025.

Enterprise EBIT impact remains rare: Only 39% report any EBIT impact attributable to AI, and most say less than 5% of their EBIT comes from AI — translating use-case wins into P&L outcomes is unsolved for most.

Agent adoption is nascent and narrow: While 62% experiment with agents, no single business function has more than 10% of respondents reporting scaled agent use — broad agentic deployment is still early-stage.

Smaller companies are structurally disadvantaged: Only 29% of firms under $100M in revenue have reached the scaling phase vs. nearly 50% of $5B+ firms — AI's compounding benefits risk becoming a large-company advantage.

Workforce impact uncertainty: Respondents are split — 32% expect workforce reductions, 43% expect no change, 13% expect increases — with expectations of decrease nearly doubling from 17% (past year actual) to 30% (next year anticipated), creating planning uncertainty for HR and operations leaders.

Risk mitigation gaps persist: Explainability is widely experienced as a risk but is not among the most mitigated — organizations are reactive rather than proactive on this dimension. High performers, despite deploying twice as many use cases, face disproportionately higher exposure to IP infringement and regulatory compliance risks.

Senior leadership engagement is inconsistently present: High performers are 3x more likely to have strong senior leadership ownership — implying that the majority of organizations lack the executive commitment needed to drive AI transformation.

Fundamentally redesign workflows — not just add AI tools on top of existing processes. Workflow redesign is identified as 'one of the strongest contributions to achieving meaningful business impact of all the factors tested.'

Set growth and innovation as AI objectives alongside efficiency. Organizations with only efficiency goals underperform those that also target growth and innovation across qualitative enterprise outcomes.
Establish defined processes for human validation of model outputs to ensure accuracy — a top differentiating practice among high performers.
Build an agile product delivery organization or enterprise-wide agile structure with well-defined delivery processes — strongly correlated with AI value realization.

Develop robust talent strategies: software engineers and data engineers are the most in-demand AI roles. Hiring for AI-related roles is a common practice among scaling organizations, especially larger ones.
Invest in technology and data infrastructure — cited as showing 'meaningful contributions to AI success' in the research.
Embed AI into business processes and track KPIs for AI solutions to drive accountability and ongoing value measurement.
Ensure senior leaders actively champion AI — role-modeling AI use and demonstrating personal commitment, not just organizational endorsement.
Commit meaningful capital: high performers allocate more than 20% of digital budgets to AI, enabling faster and broader scaling.
Use AI in more business functions simultaneously — high performers regularly use AI across marketing/sales, strategy/corporate finance, and product/service development, not just in IT.
IT service desk management via AI agents — one of the most developed and widely scaling agentic use cases across organizations.
Deep research and knowledge management via AI agents — rapidly emerging as a top-three function for AI use overall.
Contact center and customer service automation — among the most commonly reported individual AI use cases.
Content support for marketing strategy — including drafting, generating ideas, and presenting knowledge for marketing strategy creation.
Information capture, processing, and delivery via conversational interfaces — the most commonly reported category of AI use case.
Software engineering — a top function for cost benefits from AI use cases.
Manufacturing — cited as a top function for AI-driven cost benefits.
Marketing and sales — top function for AI-driven revenue increases, consistently across multiple years of McKinsey data.
Strategy and corporate finance — among functions most commonly reporting AI-driven revenue increases.
Product and service development — among functions most commonly reporting AI-driven revenue increases.

whats_ahead.content: The survey's conclusion points to three forward-looking signals: (1) As AI tools — including agents — improve and organizational AI capabilities mature, the opportunity to 'embed AI more fully into the enterprise will offer organizations new ways to capture value and create competitive advantage.' (2) Workforce impact is expected to accelerate: the share of respondents anticipating AI-related workforce reductions is projected to nearly double from a median of 17% (past year actuals) to 30% (next year expectations) across business functions. (3) The divergence between AI high performers and the rest is likely to widen — high performers are scaling faster, investing more, and redesigning more aggressively, suggesting a compounding advantage dynamic. The survey implies that the window for organizations to move from piloting to scaling is narrowing, and those that delay workflow transformation risk falling behind peers who are already embedding AI into core business processes at scale.
